explicit_text: My mother was born in Long An, Vietnam, in 1962. Vietnam’s history deeply affected my family, especially with the Fall of Saigon in 1975 when the North Communist Vietnam Party took over. My mother and uncle fled Vietnam by boat in 1981, separated from the rest of their family, seeking a safer life. Their journey from Vietnam marked the beginning of many hardships but also hope for a new future.
implicit_text: My mother was born in Long An, in Southeast Asia, in 1962. The history of that country deeply affected my family, especially with the fall of its capital city in 1975 when the northern communist party took over. My mother and uncle fled by boat in 1981, separated from the rest of their family, seeking a safer life. Their journey from their homeland marked the beginning of many hardships but also hope for a new future.

explicit_text: The Fall of Saigon on April 30, 1975, was a pivotal moment in my family’s history. It was when the North Communist Vietnam Party took over Vietnam, causing a mass exodus of about 1.5 million Vietnamese people fleeing by boat. My mother and uncle were among those who escaped, though they were separated from their family. This event set the stage for their dangerous journey and eventual resettlement in the United States.
implicit_text: The capture of the southern capital on April 30, 1975, was a pivotal moment in my family’s history. My mother and uncle were among those who escaped, though they were separated from their family. This event set the stage for their dangerous journey and eventual resettlement in North America.

explicit_text: After fleeing Vietnam in 1981, my mother and uncle arrived at a refugee camp in Malaysia called Pulau Bidong. They stayed there for about five and a half months, recovering from their treacherous journey at sea. Malaysia was a critical first stop in their path toward resettlement, providing temporary shelter before they moved on to the Philippines.

This camp was a crucial part of their journey, offering a brief respite before they were transferred to another camp in the Philippines.

explicit_text: Following their time in Malaysia, my mother and uncle were transferred to a refugee camp in the Philippines called Bataan. They stayed there for another five and a half months while their paperwork was processed for acceptance into the United States. The Philippines was an important step in their journey toward a new life in America.

explicit_text: My mother and uncle were accepted into the United States in 1982, arriving in Seattle, Washington. They were among 823,000 Vietnamese refugees accepted by the U.S. and were sponsored by a couple named Mr. and Mrs. Cotton. The United States gave my mother the opportunity to overcome language barriers, finish her degree, and open her own business. Because of her sacrifices, I have been able to pursue my passion and represent the U.S. in Taekwondo.

Because of her sacrifices, I have been able to pursue my passion and represent this nation in Taekwondo.

explicit_text: Seattle, Washington was the city where my mother and uncle first arrived when they came to the United States in 1982. It was the gateway for their new life after years in refugee camps. From Seattle, they eventually relocated to Minnesota to be closer to the Vietnamese community and friends they knew from back home.

Later, they moved from Washington to Minnesota to join a larger Vietnamese community and reconnect with friends from Vietnam.

explicit_text: In the summer of 2014, I had the honor of competing on the U.S. National Taekwondo Team. Representing the United States on this team was a proud moment for me, especially knowing my mother’s sacrifices that made this possible. Wearing USA on my back at the World Taekwondo Championships in Mexico was a dream come true and a way to give back to the country that welcomed my family.
